speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number two. Survivor is Evy Woods. The date is April 16th, 1996. We left off on the last tape. I asked for you to describe yourself as a youngster. Is there anything else you'd like to say about yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I was probably very outgoing. And while that proved a lot of fun, it also later became a dangerous situation, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite toy as a youngster?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, when we did go, finally, into hiding, I had a large doll-- a fairly good-sized doll like this. And dolls had a different meaning than they do today, you know? Children don't prize their toys. They have rooms full of toys and-- all over the place. I don't understand all this. But the doll was named Pummel. P-U-M-M-E-L, like 'pummel,' because it meant kind of chubby or whatever. And it was simply a prized doll. And that Pummel went into hiding with us, although it was a large thing to keep and trying to stay in a small space.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a pet at all as a youngster?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, people didn't keep pets. It was not even allowed. You had enough trouble feeding yourself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Pets were actually, even later by law, forbidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any dreams or ambitions for the future as a real young child, what you wanted to be when you grow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Gee. People did not live their lives-- you were a child, and you lived your childhood. And it was a more stretched out childhood that ended kind of abruptly at 16, on the average. People were considered adults at 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember having non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. By the time I was born, even my grandparents, friends, spouse, were living in a different place, as of course were my mother and father and I, although they communicated and must have occasionally seen each other. It was already dangerous for non-Jewish people to see Jewish people. That was already-- that was another law. There's always the law, so where would I-- where would I meet such people?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any incidences of antisemitism to you, personally, as a youngster?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Remember, I lived in one of the largest cities in the world-- you know, capital of Germany. You found, maybe, a little more tolerance in the average person on the street. And I just say the average, because there were so many different kinds of people. In fact, the Berlin police and-- was considered even somewhat lax. And so eventually, to help round up everybody as the time approached to get this Judenrein, they brought in the Austrian Gestapo and police to do that. They were more willing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any recall as to when the first signs of danger occurred, or things changed in your life? How old were you when things really began to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it must have been the moment we crawled through the wardrobe into the attic space. [LAUGHS] And I had a terrible cold and cough. And here was a feather bed being pushed through this panel, and I'm right behind it. And this cough is going gangbusters, and there's no medicine because Jews aren't allowed to buy medicine, you see. We had to keep stifling that cough in the down cover or in the pillow, and this went on for some time. And that altered where I was sleeping in the attic up there. But that was the traumatic thing. My toy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's see. I was not quite five. So it was March-- the first several days in March of '43, and my birthday is in June.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you personally know anything about Hitler? Had you seen him at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't understand it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had no understanding of what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And if you saw armbands on somebody with the swastika, you thought some people wore clothes like this. It didn't mean anything abnormal. I'm probably thankful that my parents never behaved fearfully. And that may have been their particular background, living in the big city, having for generations been Germans, both of them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That it felt like mine. It felt, as if in America, suddenly someone says to you, all Methodists over here. And the Methodists to say, what are you talking about? I'm an American. So you still continue to feel a certain degree of belonging. And you treat what's happening as temporary if you're an adult.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, you don't want to frighten your child. I was very lucky that I saw no signs of fear in them, which I'm sure they had to work hard to make happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's begin, I guess, chronologically. What is the memory-- first memory you have. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Things began to change. You talked about that a little bit. You were about-- is it about five years old that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, not quite five.

speaker: interviewer
text: --noticed some significant changes in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I didn't go to the kindergarten anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you told why?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. European parents don't explain everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else changed for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went into the attic, and suddenly we're walking around on double layers of Persian rugs. You know, great big thick carpeting so that nobody could hear down below.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: The attic was in the same building as the one we lived in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the circumstances? Why did you go into the attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was a patient for some time, due to the loss of a leg from being run over by a postal wagon on the street. And no medicine being permitted, so he was obviously never going to get well from his amputated leg. He was at the Jewish hospital at which my mother's sister-- eight years younger than she-- was a nurse. And the-- two things happened. The Jewish hospital was being liquidated. Excuse me. By liquidated, I mean transported out, patients and all. In fact, my mother was-- sneaked over there on the actual day that that happened to try to get a glimpse of her father one more time. And I don't know whether she succeeded or thought she did, because they had to carry him out on a stretcher. And the nurses and staff, for the most part, were rounded up at the same time. And so my mother's sister's husband, who was similar in age to my mother-- although she was only herself 21. That was unusual to be married that early. In those times, people did things unusual already. And they volunteered to go with the father and wanted us to do likewise. Well, we couldn't do that. But this is what brought some of the chaotic sensation, because we knew the way the identification system worked, that from these people, they would round up the rest of that family. That just followed. That's how it worked. Sometimes you could do it by urging them to volunteer so they could, quote, 'stay together,' which turned out to be meaningless. And my father refused to do that, so we had to go underground. We knew within so many hours, or 72 hours, 36 hours, they would come to the apartment to round us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in other words-- make sure I understand the story correctly-- your grandfather and your aunt were rounded up? Am I understanding correctly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the husband volunteered to go with father-in-law and wife. And, you know, you have to ask yourself, what would you be doing? Would you do that? Chances are most of us would.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother and father [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] agreed not to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. They refused to do that. And my father's standard sentence was, we will not deliver ourselves into the hands of the murderers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Which was very difficult-- must have been very difficult for my mother. You know, it's hard to understand one's parent because they were adults while this happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this caused you to go into your first experience in hiding. Now, can you describe the attic? What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll do my best. Actually, there's a drawing somewhere in our belongings that my mother made. I urged her to do it about 10, 20 years ago. It really consisted, essentially, of two rooms, one a little larger, one a little smaller, and with a connecting door. I have to explain that Mr. Levent, owner of the building, must have been extremely wealthy and very well-connected to the-- to the Kaiser in those times. He was just a different kind of man. And as he saw things shaping up, he had that attic unit built at his own place. The trick was to do it without the knowledge of tenants. You had over there in most buildings what was called a [NON-ENGLISH]. A [NON-ENGLISH] was a person who served mostly as your handyman person, not the person who is the manager as far as renting and so on. No, no, no-- only the physical aspects of the building and repairs and such. Well, by this time, he had completed the-- the building was, I think, upstairs completed, my mother said, maybe four years before, so say, in '39. Because he had information, because he was a man who was well connected with data and had, for instance, the non-Jewish lawyer. He got a lot of information. He had that built. Had one toilet, a small kitchen with a table and chairs for two, and everything in miniature. I couldn't tell you the square footage, but I would imagine something smaller than Anne Frank and family had from what I had seen. Much smaller. And there was one window that was covered with tar paper so that no light would come out. It was done up with electricity, the whole works. Aside from that, the Levents had two postal carrier uniforms. Think of our mailman today in some blue outfit. They had two uniforms that would fit them, and they had-- felt that if they needed to escape-- if it came to hiding in there and escape, they would put these on and go over the roofs. I don't know what they pictured at age 70, but it was still a feeling of hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said to us, well, we don't know what you will do. But they had shared that information with us maybe eight months before the happening, because my parents were worried sick. What could they do? How could they hide? Hiding always sounds easy, but only if you know people who are not Jewish. Did your parents-- are you aware of any attempts your parents may have made to try to leave the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of course. Well, and there was actually a technical problem. My father and I were German quota. We were born in Berlin, Germany. My mother was born in [? Schucken, ?] Germany, part of the Polish corridor that reverted in 1922 back to Poland. And that forced them out of that region because they were German citizens. Yes, we were actual citizens. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This disputed territory had been back and forth, and that's why people's names were spelled so oddly periodically. The Poles were in-- they would refuse your German spelling of your name and phonetically spell it out the way they do. That's why we had people whose-- in the family whose Loszinski was spelled unpronounceable for us. And my-- my grandparents and mother, who was then a little girl, went to Berlin because the brothers and sisters of each were already in Berlin, and had been doing business there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your parents tried to make some attempts to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but they couldn't, because, due to the 1922 business, my mother was now termed Polish quota. It's like saying, this is now Poland; therefore, you're a Pole. We ran into that same problem later with the Russians. They would tell me, you know, you go to this side, you're a German. Your mother goes to this side, she's Polish. Had a terrible time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you're going back to the hiding place, who knew that you were going into hiding in the apartment building? Did the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Levent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Levent, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they in hiding with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in hiding with us, but had gone in there earlier. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- apparently, they had been threatened already for deportation, because the elderly, you see. Everywhere there was a connection to the elderly, or the ill, or the mentally unbalanced, or 'flawed' human beings in addition to being Jews-- were rounded up constantly, and earlier than the workforce, like my parents who were in forced labor who were useful working for Graf Zeppelin-- my mother and my father-- for a spare in some department. Manual work, of course. But the Levents-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] lost my thread. I can't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was asking if the Levents were in hiding with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They had gone in earlier because they had been listed. And so they made themselves disappear. And it was assumed they'd been deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many people were in the attic at one time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five. Two, and then we three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, do you have any recollection of what your time was like in that attic, what you did during the day. Did you play? Did you-- could you walk around freely in the attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, quietly in socks. [LAUGHS] You know, there were all these kinds of things. But we had to eat during certain hours because we didn't want to burn that electric light that was there, for fear that somebody would see that there's a light on. Light was a-- energy was a shortage, and besides-- eventually, I mean, even by then, there were threats of the air raids being attracted by lights. My father read, and believe it or not, my father knitted during that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they entertain you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. People didn't entertain their children--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in that world, or in any of those worlds. Well, you just played however you play. In fact, I must tell you of a bizarre game we used to play on Sundays. These children that I referenced as having been removed from the kindergarten. We used to play a game called [GERMAN], and that word means 'rounding up.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So we must have known something, we children. And then we would agree. I'm the Gestapo, and you're the people we're going to pick up. I'm not joking. This is true. This was before you were in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're talking about in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, those children that were missing that I referenced. If they came to visit on a Sunday with their mothers and fathers, one or both of them, if you believe, would play that game. I mean, that is enough to make one's parents pretty scared. But I had to entertain myself, however one does that. I had some crayons. I had a little coloring book, and I had the doll and probably a few little books. I remember there was one book that every child has called [GERMAN], which was used as a basis for teaching truth, hygiene, and such. It's a picture of a young child from the 1800s, maybe even late 1700s. It's been around for years. The fingernails are grown out like Howard Hughes. The hair is bizarre and uncut for years. And the ideas is, he wouldn't let his parents cut his nails and trim his hair and so forth. And that book was filled with lessons like that. That was what you read. Everything had an application.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for food? How did you get food in and out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, the Levents, fortunately, who were financially fairly well off because they were still continuing to get money. Not their full money, but a portion of their money to stay alive. They had already stocked all this largely with tins-- things that were in tins. And that was better. We were eating better in hiding, according to my mother, than we did before the hiding on our ration cards, which had most coupons, like meat-- invalid. Bread-- invalid. Rice-- invalid. You got this ration card with nearly everything marked invalid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had a stock supply of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had stocked all that stuff in there. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- recall anything you  ate in the  attic? Who did the cooking?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that either. Probably Mrs. Levent and my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you go-- could you ever leave the attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because of this terrible cough, they had to take a chance and put me into another space by adding what I am describing was a wooden floor or a hallway all around that whole square of that building with a courtyard in the middle. And there was a door at every corner that would open and close. If you went through sections-- wanted to walk, say, the whole perimeter of the apartment building or attic. Well, every tenant had attic space aside. But many of them had nothing that they were storing. They were already run out of things. This was a building for Jews other than the post office. And so they had to put me every night into a different storage space to sleep [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because they feared the noise would carry despite the carpets.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you? Can you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, vaguely only. Vaguely, that it was being alone. But, you know, I was raised not to question anything. You just don't question anything. Why am I being left here? Your business was to keep quiet and do what your parents told you. They didn't have time for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you mom tuck you in at night there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they would.

speaker: interviewer
text: She walked you over?

speaker: interviewee
text: But the danger was, what if I had to get up or go to bathroom or something? But you just don't. I learned to hold it for decades. And then I discovered, if you don't eat or drink much, you don't need to go at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: In fact, the drinking went on probably until about five years ago. If I didn't drink, I didn't need to go. The magic bladder. And so that started already then, because when are you-- when are you going to go to the bathroom? These are simple needs, but they are necessary. So they would bring you back to their space during the day--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and then walk you at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd go in there in my nightgown. And the idea was, if something did happen to them, I would be found up there. But nobody would know that I was a Jewish child. How were they going to know? I wouldn't be in the same attic space.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the other people in the building. Were they mostly Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to them? Were they not in hiding, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mentioned that we actually were in a flat, and the rooms were allocated to people. OK? You didn't have your whole flat. I remember there was a woman with blond hair. I even still remember her hair and all because it was so unusual to see Jewish people with blond hair. Even to us it was unusual, even though I had the blond hair. But my parents didn't. My mother's sister and all these people didn't. Well, this woman's name was Trudchen-- which means Little Trudy-- Traploviz I can find her name now in the black book of all the dead German Jews, in the two volumes that I have. It has about 340,000 names in it. Trudchen Traploviz, I believe, is the one who jumped out of the window when they came to arrest them. In other words, they would come. There would be that dreaded knock after 7:00 at night, and you knew somebody is being rounded up, Since this was not just our place to live but of a number of others, it could be for someone else. And when that happened, I understand that, like so many people, one was arrested, the other one jumped out the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you recall being in the attic and hearing the banging of the doors?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the attic, banging on the doors-- no, there were no doors in the attic, but one steel door.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, so you couldn't hear other people being rounded up? You didn't know who was going to be gone the next day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because these windows went out into the courtyard of the post office-- that huge courtyard with the cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go out on the street this time you were in the attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my mother did, because, you see, it was easier for women. Men could simply not be seen. Remember, there's a war effort on, and everyone is in uniform, is serving in the service. Let's even forget the Jewish issue. The fact that a man should walk around without a uniform immediately would arouse someone. And citizen's arrests were allowed. You're not wearing your star. And by this time, there were hardly any Jews in Berlin. We were already among the last. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyone could ask you your ID papers. Well, we didn't have any. We'd burned them and the star just before the attic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the star? Do you remember yourself having a star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. No, because there-- you know, there are so many laws, it turns out, as you meet other people, now, from these times and in other countries. The rules were not uniformly applied. I've been undergoing surprise even this year on hearing that the laws being turned around to be opposite in many other cities of Germany. For instance, in Poland and a number of other countries, Jews had to wear the star no matter how old they were. And we've all seen pictures of children in the streets, in the ghettos, with stars on. And these little children were no more than two years old, barely walking. In Germany, supposedly, but in Berlin for certain, no child under six years wore that star. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, that's why I didn't receive any restitution from Germany, because you had to have worn the star or been in the camp to receive restitution.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very long. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Weeks? Months?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's just weeks. It was just weeks, because the [INAUDIBLE]-- and I like to use the people's names in case their relatives are still out there, they should know-- and Mr. Maletski-- that name will come up again later. Mr. Maletski had become suspicious by some new plaster in contrast to the old plaster along the attic wall, which is where-- behind which the little unit was. Although it wasn't that new-- it wasn't like last week's plaster-- it looked different to him. And he brought the Gestapo up there. Gestapo-- by Gestapo, I always mean plainclothesmen, no uniforms. And we were sitting and eating our food. It was our turn to eat at this little table. And suddenly, it was complete silence. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we heard, [GERMAN], which is an improper way of talking German. I mean, it's worse than colloquial. It's grammatically-- and in that country, you're judged by how you speak, still to this day. It meant, 'here, somewhere, is where they must be.' So we heard that, and we stopped eating. And we were just quiet. The Levents were in their little room. We had the somewhat larger room. There were little cots on which we slept. And everyone just stood stock still. And it was still daylight, I remember, because during the daylight, we could take-- let some of the regular light in. And the next thing we know, there's a shot, because the hiding place-- the attic-- had not only the wardrobe way of getting in with a clad iron door-- or a steel door-- with a lock at the very bottom of it, which we kept locked when we were in. It would take some effort for them to break that down. They would probably break down everything surrounding the door, and the door would remain standing. And we had what we called an emergency exit. A desk had, on the inside of the little unit, been placed against the wall. And underneath it was the hole. And from the other side, a bunch of rubble had been placed, like ashes, broken dishes. And of course, one of the Gestapo men had shot into that idly. [INAUDIBLE] Well, that just took minutes. Because when-- when you shoot into a pile like that, idly or not, what happened was that the whole pile collapsed and exposed the exit hole that was supposed to be the emergency exit if they came in through the wardrobe and all that. And that's-- I've since heard other wardrobe stories. Other people had the same ideas that came to all of us. Well, in came-- I don't know if it was four or five plainclothesmen. And for years, I avoided all people with green Tyrolean hats. Remember, hats used to be worn by men. And it-- particularly, if they had a little brush up here, like a shaving brush almost-- feathers, looking like a Tyrolean. And coats and all that. And there they stood with their revolvers in their hand.
